title: 10_cors_server_headers.js
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Fix CORS at the Right Layer

The React frontend at `https://app.example.com` calls a Node/Express API at `https://api.example.com`. The browser blocks every request with a CORS error. A junior engineer added a `mode: 'no-cors'` option to the fetch call to "fix" it — the error message disappeared, but now all responses come back as opaque responses with no readable data, and the app shows blank results instead of content.

CORS is a *browser* security policy enforced by checking the response headers the server sends. The fix must be on the server: the API must respond with the correct `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header so the browser permits the frontend to read the response. A client-side workaround cannot grant permissions that the server has not issued.

Fix the Express server to send the required CORS headers. Handle the preflight `OPTIONS` request that browsers send before cross-origin requests with custom headers.
traceback:
Browser console:
  Access to fetch at 'https://api.example.com/api/users' from origin
  'https://app.example.com' has been blocked by CORS policy:
  No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present on the requested resource.

Client-side "fix" applied (mode: 'no-cors'):
  fetch('/api/users', { mode: 'no-cors' }).then(r => r.json())
  → TypeError: Failed to execute 'json' on 'Response': body is unusable.
  Opaque responses have type 'opaque' and their body cannot be read.
correctOutput:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, OPTIONS
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type
Content-Type: application/json

{"users":[{"id":1,"name":"Alice"},{"id":2,"name":"Bob"}]}

options.label: Add a CORS middleware that sets the required headers and handles OPTIONS preflight requests
options.resultLog:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, OPTIONS
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type
Content-Type: application/json

{"users":[{"id":1,"name":"Alice"},{"id":2,"name":"Bob"}]}
options.rationale: CORS permission is granted by the server via response headers. `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` tells the browser which origins may read the response; setting it to the exact frontend origin allows the read and rejects all others. The `OPTIONS` handler responds to the browser's preflight request — sent before any cross-origin request with custom headers or non-simple methods — with 204 and the allow headers, so the actual request is permitted to proceed. The specific origin is safer than the wildcard `*`, which would allow any origin to call the API.

options.label: Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to * to allow any origin to call the API
options.resultLog:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *

{"users":[{"id":1,"name":"Alice"},{"id":2,"name":"Bob"}]}

Note: The CORS error is gone, but any origin on the internet can now call this API
and read its responses — including `credentials: 'include'` requests are blocked by
browsers when the header is *, so auth flows will break in the next sprint.
options.rationale: The wildcard `*` fixes the CORS error and the test passes, but it grants read access to *any* origin on the internet. For a public, truly anonymous API this may be acceptable. For this API, which will need to add cookie-based authentication (credentials), `*` creates two problems: browsers refuse to include credentials with `*` responses (they require a specific origin for credentialed requests), and it opens the API to any website that wants to call it. Allowlisting the exact frontend origin is the safer default for authenticated APIs.

options.label: Add a proxy config to the frontend build tool so requests look same-origin to the browser
options.resultLog:
Development: CORS error gone (dev server proxies the request, same-origin)
Production:  Access blocked — CORS policy: No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header.

Proxy configs in Vite/CRA/webpack are dev-server only. They are not included in the
production build. The API at api.example.com still has no CORS headers.
options.rationale: A dev-server proxy makes API calls look same-origin in development because the build tool's dev server forwards them. This is a legitimate development convenience, but it does nothing in production — the built JavaScript runs in the user's browser, which calls `api.example.com` directly. The CORS error returns on the first production deploy. CORS must be configured on the server that serves the API responses; no client-side or build-tool workaround can substitute for it.

hints: CORS is enforced by the *browser*, which checks whether the *server's response* includes permission headers before letting JavaScript read the response. A client-side fix (`mode: 'no-cors'`, a proxy config) cannot add headers to a response the server did not include. Where should the fix live?

The server needs to send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com` on every response, plus handle the preflight `OPTIONS` request that the browser sends before cross-origin POST or requests with custom headers. Add a middleware that sets these headers and returns 204 for OPTIONS.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism. When JavaScript on `https://app.example.com` calls `https://api.example.com`, the browser sends the request but *withholds the response* from the JavaScript code unless the response includes an `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header that permits the requesting origin. The server received the request just fine — CORS is entirely a browser-side check on the server's response headers.

`mode: 'no-cors'` tells the browser to make the request without checking for CORS headers, but in exchange it returns an *opaque* response: a Response object with no status, no headers, and no readable body. JavaScript cannot extract any data from it. The error message disappeared but the data access was still blocked — just silently.

### Why the fix is correct
Adding a middleware that sets `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com` grants the browser permission to let the frontend JavaScript read the response. The `OPTIONS` preflight handler is required for POST requests and any request with custom headers (like `Content-Type: application/json`): before sending the actual request, the browser sends an `OPTIONS` request to check whether the method and headers are allowed. Without a 204 response to that preflight, the actual request never fires.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and you named the right mental model: CORS is a browser check on server response headers, so the fix belongs on the server. The `*` wildcard distractor is the path-of-least-resistance answer — it silences the error and passes the basic test, but breaks credentialed requests and opens the API to any origin. Recognizing that specific-origin allowlisting is the safer default for an API that will eventually need authentication shows you're thinking two sprints ahead. The proxy distractor is the one that causes the most confusion in teams because it "works" in development for months before anyone tries a production deploy. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## CORS: What It Is, Where It Lives, and How to Fix It

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing is a browser security mechanism that controls whether JavaScript on one origin can read responses from a different origin. It is purely a browser-enforced policy — servers receive cross-origin requests normally; the browser is the gatekeeper that decides whether to hand the response to JavaScript.

### Origins and the same-origin policy
An *origin* is the combination of scheme, host, and port: `https://app.example.com:443`. Two URLs have the same origin only if all three match exactly. `https://api.example.com` is a different origin from `https://app.example.com` even though they share a domain suffix. Any request from one origin to another is a *cross-origin* request and triggers CORS.

### Simple vs. preflighted requests
Browsers split cross-origin requests into two categories:

**Simple requests** (GET/HEAD/POST with only `Content-Type: text/plain | application/x-www-form-urlencoded | multipart/form-data`) go directly to the server. The browser checks the response headers; if `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` matches the requesting origin, JavaScript gets the response.

**Preflighted requests** (POST with `Content-Type: application/json`, any custom header, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) first send an HTTP `OPTIONS` request to the server asking: "Will you allow a cross-origin request with these headers and method?" The server must respond with the appropriate `Access-Control-Allow-*` headers and a 2xx status. Only then does the browser send the real request.

### The required headers
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
```

For credentialed requests (cookies, HTTP auth), you must also set:
```
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
```
And `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` must be a specific origin, not `*` — browsers reject credentialed requests with a wildcard origin.

### Why client-side workarounds fail
- `mode: 'no-cors'` — makes the request but returns an opaque response with no readable body or status.
- Dev-server proxy — only active in development; the production browser calls the API directly.
- Setting response headers in the *browser* — not possible; headers are set by the server.

### Using the cors npm package
For Express, the `cors` package handles the middleware boilerplate:
```js
const cors = require('cors');
app.use(cors({
  origin: 'https://app.example.com',
  methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],
  allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization'],
  credentials: true,
}));
```

### Common variations of this bug
- Setting `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` only on 2xx responses but not on 4xx/5xx — preflight returns 204 but the actual error response is blocked.
- Dynamic origin allowlisting that echoes the `Origin` header without validation — effectively `*` but with credentials allowed, a security hole.
- `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` missing `Authorization` — auth headers cause preflight to fail even though the origin is allowed.
- CORS configured at the app level but an upstream load balancer or CDN strips the headers before the browser sees them.
